questions.stem: These H&E micrographs of a glandular adenocarcinoma progress from Grade 1 to Grade 3. What does this histologic progression primarily represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transformation of malignant tissue back to benign glands

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increasing glandular differentiation as the tumor advances

choices.label: C
choices.text: Progressive increase in normal stromal architecture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Progressive loss of glandular differentiation with increasing tumor grade

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loss of the capacity to metastasize at higher grades

explanation.summary: Tumor grading reflects differentiation. Well-differentiated (Grade 1) tumors form discrete glands resembling normal tissue, whereas high-grade (Grade 3) tumors lose glandular architecture and grow as sheets of pleomorphic cells, correlating with more aggressive clinical behavior.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tumor grading reflects differentiation. Well-differentiated (Grade 1) tumors form discrete glands resembling normal tissue, whereas high-grade (Grade 3) tumors lose glandular architecture and grow as sheets of pleomorphic cells, correlating with more aggressive clinical behavior.

questions.stem: This diagram depicts progressive stages of a lung malignancy defined by tumor size, nodal involvement, and distant spread. Which classification system underlies this staging scheme?

explanation.summary: Solid-tumor stage is defined by the TNM system: extent of the primary Tumor, regional lymph Node involvement, and distant Metastasis. The escalating panels show larger tumors with progressive nodal then distant spread, the hallmark of increasing TNM stage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Solid-tumor stage is defined by the TNM system: extent of the primary Tumor, regional lymph Node involvement, and distant Metastasis. The escalating panels show larger tumors with progressive nodal then distant spread, the hallmark of increasing TNM stage.

questions.stem: A sharply demarcated, raised, shiny erythematous plaque on the lower leg with a well-defined elevated border. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Erysipelas is a superficial dermal streptococcal infection producing a well-demarcated, raised, indurated bright-red plaque. The sharp, elevated border distinguishes it from cellulitis, which involves deeper tissue and has poorly defined margins.
explanation.whyCorrect: Erysipelas is a superficial dermal streptococcal infection producing a well-demarcated, raised, indurated bright-red plaque. The sharp, elevated border distinguishes it from cellulitis, which involves deeper tissue and has poorly defined margins.

questions.stem: A young child with widespread superficial skin denudation and peeling of large sheets of epidermis, sparing the mucous membranes. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: SSSS results from Staphylococcus aureus exfoliative toxins that cleave desmoglein-1 in the superficial epidermis, causing sheet-like desquamation. It predominates in young children and spares mucous membranes, unlike TEN/SJS, which cause deeper full-thickness necrosis with prominent mucosal involvement.
explanation.whyCorrect: SSSS results from Staphylococcus aureus exfoliative toxins that cleave desmoglein-1 in the superficial epidermis, causing sheet-like desquamation. It predominates in young children and spares mucous membranes, unlike TEN/SJS, which cause deeper full-thickness necrosis with prominent mucosal involvement.

questions.stem: An elderly patient with a rapidly spreading area of dusky discoloration, hemorrhagic change, and central skin necrosis on the trunk. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Necrotizing fasciitis is a rapidly progressive infection of deep fascia and subcutaneous tissue producing dusky skin, hemorrhagic bullae, and necrosis often out of proportion to overlying findings. It is a surgical emergency requiring urgent, aggressive debridement.
explanation.whyCorrect: Necrotizing fasciitis is a rapidly progressive infection of deep fascia and subcutaneous tissue producing dusky skin, hemorrhagic bullae, and necrosis often out of proportion to overlying findings. It is a surgical emergency requiring urgent, aggressive debridement.

questions.stem: Intraoperative photograph of a limb opened along its length, exposing subcutaneous tissue and muscle, with surrounding dusky skin. This surgical management is most characteristic of which condition?

explanation.summary: The mainstay of treatment for necrotizing soft-tissue infection is aggressive surgical exploration and debridement, shown here as a long fasciotomy exposing deep tissues. Antibiotics alone are inadequate because thrombosed vessels prevent drug delivery to necrotic tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: The mainstay of treatment for necrotizing soft-tissue infection is aggressive surgical exploration and debridement, shown here as a long fasciotomy exposing deep tissues. Antibiotics alone are inadequate because thrombosed vessels prevent drug delivery to necrotic tissue.

questions.stem: Views of a foot showing punched-out ulcers with necrotic bases located over the plantar pressure points and toes. What is the most likely underlying cause?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Venous stasis ulcer of the medial ankle

explanation.summary: Neuropathic (diabetic) ulcers form at weight-bearing plantar pressure points such as the metatarsal heads, toes, and heel because sensory loss permits repetitive unnoticed trauma. They are typically painless with a punched-out appearance and surrounding callus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neuropathic (diabetic) ulcers form at weight-bearing plantar pressure points such as the metatarsal heads, toes, and heel because sensory loss permits repetitive unnoticed trauma. They are typically painless with a punched-out appearance and surrounding callus.

questions.stem: Transient branching, fern-like erythematous streaks appear on the skin of a patient found collapsed outdoors during a thunderstorm. What do these markings indicate?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lichtenberg figure from a lightning strike

explanation.summary: Lichtenberg figures are pathognomonic, transient fern-like or feathering skin marks produced by a lightning strike, thought to arise from electron discharge tracking along superficial vessels. They fade within hours to days and are not true burns.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lichtenberg figures are pathognomonic, transient fern-like or feathering skin marks produced by a lightning strike, thought to arise from electron discharge tracking along superficial vessels. They fade within hours to days and are not true burns.

questions.stem: Both hands of this patient show a dusky bluish-gray discoloration most pronounced in the nail beds. What does this finding represent?

explanation.summary: Cyanosis is a bluish discoloration of skin and nail beds caused by increased deoxygenated hemoglobin (>5 g/dL) in capillary blood. It is best appreciated in the nail beds and mucous membranes and signals hypoxemia or poor perfusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cyanosis is a bluish discoloration of skin and nail beds caused by increased deoxygenated hemoglobin (>5 g/dL) in capillary blood. It is best appreciated in the nail beds and mucous membranes and signals hypoxemia or poor perfusion.

questions.stem: This clinical photograph shows a hand in which several digits are shrunken, black, dry, and sharply demarcated from adjacent viable pink tissue. What process is illustrated?

explanation.summary: Dry gangrene results from arterial ischemia producing coagulative necrosis; the affected tissue becomes black, desiccated ('mummified'), and sharply demarcated from viable skin, without the edema, foul odor, or infection that characterize wet or gas gangrene.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dry gangrene results from arterial ischemia producing coagulative necrosis; the affected tissue becomes black, desiccated ('mummified'), and sharply demarcated from viable skin, without the edema, foul odor, or infection that characterize wet or gas gangrene.

questions.stem: This forensic close-up of a facial wound shows irregular, abraded, contused margins; the arrows indicate intact strands of tissue bridging across the wound gap. What type of wound is this?

explanation.summary: Tissue bridging (intact nerves/vessels/connective strands crossing the defect), together with abraded, bruised, irregular margins, is the hallmark of a laceration caused by blunt force; sharp-force incised and stab wounds have clean, everted margins without bridging.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tissue bridging (intact nerves/vessels/connective strands crossing the defect), together with abraded, bruised, irregular margins, is the hallmark of a laceration caused by blunt force; sharp-force incised and stab wounds have clean, everted margins without bridging.

questions.stem: This cross-sectional skin diagram marks a graded series of horizontal planes descending through epidermis, dermis, subcutaneous fat, and muscle. This scheme is used to classify what?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Levels of a surgical skin graft harvest

explanation.summary: The planes (superficial, superficial partial-thickness, deep partial-thickness, full-thickness) correspond to how deep tissue destruction extends, which is exactly how burns are classified; depth determines healing potential and management.
explanation.whyCorrect: The planes (superficial, superficial partial-thickness, deep partial-thickness, full-thickness) correspond to how deep tissue destruction extends, which is exactly how burns are classified; depth determines healing potential and management.
